nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, slightly acidic blackwater and clearwater streams and tributaries within the Meta River basin of Colombia, with sandy or leaf-littered bottoms where the species forages along the substrate.

decorPreferences: fine smooth sand substrate to protect sensitive barbels
leaf litter (Indian almond, oak leaves) for biotope authenticity and tannin buffering
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
low-light plants (Java fern, Anubias, Amazon sword)
open foraging areas interspersed with shaded retreats

diet.notes: The Pink Corydoras is a benthic forager that accepts a wide range of sinking foods. A staple diet of high-quality sinking catfish pellets, micro-pellets, and Corydoras wafers works well as the base, supplemented with live or frozen bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, and Tubifex worms offered two to three times per week. It will also take high-quality crushed flake food that sinks to the bottom. The species should not be relied upon as a tank cleaner; targeted feeding at lights-out is the best way to ensure each fish receives adequate nutrition. Its small mouth is not well suited to coarse or large-chunk foods.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) in spacious tanks
avoidWith: large catfish that may compete for bottom space
fish requiring significantly different water parameters

summary: The Pink Corydoras is a small, sociable catfish from Colombia's Meta River basin, recognized by its pinkish-orange body and iridescent sheen. It thrives in groups of six or more and is straightforward to keep in a soft-water community tank. Its pectoral fin spines can deliver a mild venom when the fish is stressed.
careNotes: The Pink Corydoras is a small, bottom-dwelling catfish endemic to the Río Meta drainage in Colombia, where it inhabits slow-moving soft acidic waters. In the aquarium it is peaceful and widely considered hardy, making it well-suited to beginner and intermediate community setups provided water quality is maintained. Keep in groups of at least six — social cohesion reduces stress and encourages natural foraging behaviour. A fine smooth sand substrate (minimum 3 cm depth) is strongly recommended; coarse or sharp gravel can erode the delicate barbels that are essential for locating food. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–12) best reflects the species’ wild habitat; however, aquarium-bred specimens show some tolerance for near-neutral conditions. Dim lighting and the addition of leaf litter or tannin-staining driftwood are appreciated. This species bears pectoral and dorsal fin spines capable of delivering a mild venom when the fish is stressed; never net bare-handed and use a container or deep net to transfer specimens. The Pink Corydoras should not be treated as a “tank cleaner” — it requires its own dedicated sinking foods and will not thrive on leftovers alone. Copper-based medications are known to be poorly tolerated by callichthyid catfishes; avoid their use in tanks housing this species. A 2024 phylogenomic revision resurrected the genus Hoplisoma and reclassified this species as Hoplisoma axelrodi; the new name is increasingly appearing in scientific literature though Corydoras axelrodi remains prevalent in the hobby trade.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable with moderate effort. Condition a separate breeding group (2–3 males per female) on varied live and frozen foods for several weeks. Trigger spawning by performing a large (30–40%) water change with slightly cooler (2–3°C lower), well-oxygenated water to simulate seasonal rainfall cues. Eggs are deposited in clutches of 15–30 on plant leaves, aquarium glass, or other smooth surfaces. Parents show no egg guarding and will consume eggs if left in the breeding tank; remove adults promptly after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 24°C. Free-swimming fry accept microworms and newly hatched Artemia nauplii from first feeding; progress to finely crushed micro-pellets as they grow. Maintain excellent water quality during the fry-rearing phase.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras axelrodi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Río Meta, Colombia), adult size (45–50 mm SL), water parameters (22–26°C, pH 5.0–7.0, 18–215 ppm hardness), substrate requirements (fine sand), diet (omnivore, sinking foods), behaviour (peaceful, gregarious, groups of 4–6+), breeding triggers (cooler water change, increased oxygenation), egg hatch time (3–4 days), and note on mildly toxic pectoral spine secretions.

additional.name: FishBase — Hoplisoma axelrodi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 4.2 cm SL male/unsexed), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dH), native range (Meta River basin, Colombia), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes, Corydoradinae), omnivore diet classification, IUCN status Vulnerable (VU, B2ab(iii,v), assessed 2020). FishBase now lists species as Hoplisoma axelrodi per 2024 Dias et al. revision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide confirming venomous pectoral spines (“sharp spines in their fins that can sometimes produce a mild venom when stressed”), smooth sand/gravel substrate preference, group of 6+ requirement, 20-gallon minimum for most varieties, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.5–7.8. Supplementary source; no species-specific C. axelrodi page available.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Pink Corydoras (Corydoras axelrodi)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 4–5 cm, temperature 22–27°C, pH 6.1–7.5, hardness 7–20°N, native range Río Meta (Colombia), omnivore diet (flake, pellets, algae wafers, bloodworms, brine shrimp), peaceful temperament, lifespan 5–6 years, breeding via cooler water changes, eggs hatch ~1 week, fry fed infusoria then Artemia nauplii.

additional.name: Dias et al. 2024 — Corydoradinae revision (via Aquarium Glaser / Tropical Fish Keeping UK summaries)
additional.notes: Summary of Dias, Tencatt, Roxo, Britto et al. (2024), “Phylogenomic Analyses in the Complex Neotropical Subfamily Corydoradinae”, Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society. Confirms reclassification of Corydoras axelrodi as Hoplisoma axelrodi via resurrection of genus Hoplisoma Swainson 1838. Hoplisoma represents lineage 9 of Corydoradinae and is now the largest genus in the subfamily.

fish.breedingNotes: The Pink Corydoras is an egg-scatterer whose spawning is typically triggered by large, cooler water changes. A separate breeding tank is recommended, as adults will consume eggs if not removed promptly after spawning. Eggs generally hatch within three to four days, and the fry accept microworms and Artemia nauplii from first feeding.
